skillName: Multiply and divide integers and decimals by powers of 10
misconceptionSummary: Students shift digits the wrong number of places, often shifting once for ×100 instead of twice.

steps.narration: The student shifted one place but one hundred has two zeros — two places are needed. Zero point zero six becomes six, not zero point six.

visuals.ruleText: Count the zeros in the power of 10 — that is how many places to shift

steps.narration: The number of zeros in the power of ten tells you exactly how many places to shift. One hundred has two zeros — shift two places. One thousand has three zeros — shift three places.
Shift zero point zero six two places to the left: first to zero point six, then to six. The answer is six — a whole number.

visuals.label: 100 has 2 zeros → shift 2 places left → 0.06 becomes 6

steps.narration: Six. Count the zeros in the power of ten — then shift exactly that many places. No more, no fewer.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 0.06 × 100 = 0.6 (shifted only 1 place). 100 has two zeros — shift two places left: 0.06 → 0.6 → 6. Always count the zeros.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Count the zeros in the power of ten — that is your shift distance. 100 has two zeros, so shift two places. 0.06 becomes 6, not 0.6.
